Critics and observers suggest that the discovery of the pills, combined with the broader scandal, portrays the president as being disconnected from the public.

Search crews in the southern Indian Ocean have failed to relocate faint underwater signals that were consistent with the black box recorders of Malaysia Airlines Flight MH370. The signals, which were detected by a towed pinger locator on the Australian Defence Vessel Ocean Shield late Saturday and early Sunday, lasted for over two hours and 13 minutes respectively. While the signals were considered a promising lead, the search operation is at a critical juncture as the locator beacons on the flight data and cockpit voice recorders are reaching the end of their expected 30-day battery life. The search area covers approximately 30,000 square miles, with satellite data suggesting the Boeing 777, which vanished on March 8 with 239 people on board, went down roughly 1,680 km northwest of Perth, Australia. The black boxes normally emit a frequency of 37.5 kilohertz, though the detected signals were 33.3 kilohertz; officials noted that frequencies can drift in older equipment or be affected by extreme pressure and battery age. Angus Houston, head of the search operation, emphasized the importance of confirming the signals before deploying the Bluefin-21 autonomous underwater vehicle. Because the submersible "crawls" along the seafloor and has a narrow search field, narrowing the area is essential to avoid a lengthy and difficult search in deep water. There was a slight discrepancy in communication regarding the sub's deployment, as acting Prime Minister Warren Truss initially stated it would be launched on Tuesday, while Houston noted the need for more data first. The search involves up to 14 ships and 14 planes. While 133 aerial missions have been completed, no debris from the aircraft has been found. Surface searches will continue even if the black boxes are located to find evidence regarding the cause of the disappearance, which investigators believe may have involved a deliberate diversion, though mechanical issues have not been ruled out.

Kate Downing, a member of the Palo Alto Planning and Transportation Commission and co-founder of the community group Palo Alto Forward, has resigned from her position to move her family to Santa Cruz. In her resignation letter, Downing cites the astronomical cost of housing in Palo Alto as the primary reason for her departure, stating that even as a corporate attorney with a husband who is a software engineer, she cannot afford to stay and raise a family. She noted that while her current rental costs $6,200 a month, purchasing a similar home would cost approximately $2.7 million, resulting in annual payments of $146,127—an entire professional's income before taxes. Downing criticized the Palo Alto City Council for ignoring resident demands for more affordable housing, noting that over 1,000 residents signed a petition urging the city to prioritize housing. She argued that the current development trajectory favors offices over residential units, creating a jobs-housing imbalance that affects the entire Bay Area. She specifically recommended measures such as allowing two-story housing in mixed-use developments, legalizing duplexes, easing restrictions on "granny units," enforcing minimum density requirements to favor apartments over penthouses, and allowing housing to be built above retail spaces like the Stanford shopping center. The documents highlight a broader trend of residents, including teachers, first responders, and service workers, being priced out of the area. Public surveys indicate that "cost of housing" is a top concern for residents, with 76 percent of respondents in one poll listing it as an extremely or very serious problem. Downing expressed concern that Palo Alto is becoming a "hollowed out museum" for wealthy investors and executives, losing its community spirit and the ability for young families to put down roots. The resignation comes ahead of a City Council election where several candidates have pledged to prioritize housing options.

Prince William and Duchess Kate Middleton have adopted a three-month-old male cocker spaniel puppy. The couple acquired the dog in early December and have been settling him into their rented farmhouse in North Wales. The puppy is the son of Kate’s mother, Carole Middleton, who is a fan of the breed. While the couple has confirmed the adoption, they have instructed palace aides to keep the dog's name private. The new puppy is the couple's first dog together and replaces Prince William’s black labrador, Widgeon, who died approximately two years ago. The adoption follows a short trial period during which the couple initially expressed concerns about their readiness to care for a pet. The puppy's arrival is noted as timely, as William is scheduled for a six-week deployment to the Falkland Islands, during which Kate will reside primarily at Kensington Palace with the dog. The documents highlight a long history of the Royal Family embracing dogs, noting that formal portraits have featured various breeds since the 17th century. Notable historical examples include Queen Victoria’s collie, Noble, and King Edward VII’s terrier, Caesar. The current Queen is famously associated with corgis and dorgis. The cocker spaniel breed is characterized as a friendly, compact family pet. While the dog's name remains undisclosed, betting firms have speculated on names such as Charlie, Bouncer, and Fenton.

AMC Theatres has scrapped its proposal to allow moviegoers to text during films following a significant backlash from the public. The plan, suggested by CEO Adam Aron to attract millennials, was met with overwhelming negative feedback on social media, with many patrons threatening to boycott the chain. Aron stated that the company listened to the audience and that texting will not be allowed in any AMC auditoriums in the foreseeable future. The proposal was part of a broader strategy by Aron, who joined AMC in early 2016, to modernize the company and appeal to younger demographics. Aron argued that because millennials live on their phones, the company needed to find ways to accommodate them, such as potentially creating specific "texting-friendly" auditoriums. However, the move conflicted with general public sentiment; a Pew Research Center survey found that 95% of Americans believe using a smartphone in a movie theater is inappropriate. In addition to the texting controversy, AMC is pursuing several other growth initiatives. The company announced an agreement to acquire Carmike Cinemas, a move intended to make AMC the world’s largest movie theater chain. Aron also plans to invest more than $1 billion to enhance theater systems and food options. Furthermore, AMC is reportedly close to a deal with Screening Room, a startup that offers movies for home viewing via a set-top box. While the company is experimenting with new ways to reach consumers, Aron emphasized that maintaining a high-quality theater experience remains a bedrock principle of the company.

On July 20, 2017, a Nevada parole board unanimously voted to grant O.J. Simpson parole after he served nine years of a nine-to-33-year sentence for an armed robbery and kidnapping in Las Vegas. Simpson, who is 70 years old, expressed regret for the 2007 crime, stating he had no intent to commit a crime and was misled by associates. During the hearing, Simpson was described as a model prisoner with a discipline-free record, and he received support from his daughter, Arnelle Simpson, and robbery victim Bruce Fromong, who testified that he had forgiven him. The 2007 conviction stemmed from an incident at the Palace Station Hotel & Casino where Simpson and associates used threats and guns to reclaim sports memorabilia. While Simpson claimed he was merely attempting to retrieve personal heirlooms and was misled by his associates, one of those associates, Tom Riccio, claimed that Simpson orchestrated the robbery and gathered armed men. Simpson’s legal team had previously argued that his sentencing was a form of "payback" for his 1995 acquittal in the murders of Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald Goldman. Simpson's history includes a 1995 "not guilty" verdict for the murders of his ex-wife and Goldman, followed by a $33 million civil wrongful death judgment in 1997. He also faced a 2001 road rage trial, where he was found not guilty. Following his parole, Simpson plans to move to his home in Florida.

Actor Scott Baio, a prominent supporter of Donald Trump, filed a police report alleging that Nancy Mack, the wife of Red Hot Chili Peppers drummer Chad Smith, physically attacked him during a function in Thousand Oaks, California. The incident occurred at an event hosted by an elementary school attended by children from both families. According to Baio, the confrontation began when Mack berated and cursed him regarding his support for Trump, specifically referencing Trump’s "grab 'em by the pu**y" comments. Baio claims that after he asked her to remain quiet for the sake of the children, Mack continued to scream the comment and then grabbed, shook, and pushed him. The Ventura County Sheriff’s Department is investigating the incident, and Mack is listed as a battery suspect. Sources close to Mack claim she was merely mimicking how Trump hugs women and intended no actual harm. The confrontation highlights a sharp political divide between the two parties. Baio is a known celebrity supporter of the president-elect, while Chad Smith has been vocal in his opposition, criticizing Trump supporters as "uneducated whites" and describing the president-elect as a "sexual predator." While many celebrities expressed plans to leave the country following Trump's election, Baio remains in the U.S. as a vocal supporter.

Rep. Jim Jordan is facing accusations from former Ohio State University wrestlers that he was aware of sexual misconduct by team doctor Richard Strauss during the 1990s but failed to act. Jordan, who served as an assistant wrestling coach from 1986 to 1994, has repeatedly denied these claims, stating he had no knowledge of any abuse. However, several former athletes have contradicted his statements. One wrestler, Shawn Dailey, stated that Strauss’s behavior was common knowledge in the locker room and that he participated in conversations regarding the abuse. Another wrestler, Dunyasha Yetts, claimed he directly reported an incident to Jordan and head coach Russ Hellickson in the 1990s. Mark Coleman, a former UFC world champion and Jordan’s former roommate on wrestling trips, asserted that it was impossible for Jordan to be unaware of the situation. Mike DiSabato, whose allegations prompted the university's investigation, called Jordan a "liar" and noted that Jordan asked him to stay out of the matter when DiSabato first reached out this year. The university opened an investigation in April into Strauss, who served as a team physician from 1981 to 1995 and died by suicide in 2005. The law firm Perkins Coie is overseeing the probe and has interviewed over 150 witnesses. Reports indicate that athletes from 14 different sports have reported abuse by Strauss, with some estimates suggesting up to 2,000 athletes were affected. While Jordan’s spokesman stated that the congressman has not been contacted by investigators for an interview, the law firm representing the university claims they have attempted to contact his office multiple times. Jordan has received public support from President Donald Trump, who expressed 100 percent belief in the congressman's denials. The university is continuing to investigate what leaders at the time knew and what actions were taken.

On January 14, 2017, a 29-year-old woman was rescued after being locked in the trunk of a moving vehicle by her boyfriend. The incident began around 4 a.m. when the woman called 911 from Raleigh, stating she had been kidnapped and placed in the trunk of a car traveling east on I-40 toward the I-95 interchange. During the initial call, the woman was too upset to be easily understood, and the connection was subsequently lost. Tim Medlin, a shift supervisor for the Johnston County Emergency 911 Communications Center, attempted to call the woman back multiple times without success. Medlin then used an office flip phone to send a text message to the woman. She responded, confirming she needed help and providing the name of her boyfriend, 32-year-old Nicholas Mattevi, and stating she believed the car was headed toward Fayetteville. With the information provided, authorities contacted Verizon Wireless to locate the phone. Fayetteville police spotted a red Mustang and rescued the woman, who was found unharmed. Mattevi was arrested around 5 a.m. and charged with the misdemeanor of false imprisonment. He was later released on a $10,000 bond. Jason Barbour, the director of the Johnston County 911 center, noted that while the center can receive text messages, it currently lacks the capability to initiate them from their primary systems. He stated that Medlin's use of a personal device was a successful example of technology being used in an emergency, and he expressed a desire to update the center's systems to allow for initiated texting. Medlin's supervisor indicated plans to purchase a smartphone for the team to provide a keyboard for future emergencies.

The United States is transitioning to a chip-enabled credit card processing system, utilizing EMV microchips (Europay, MasterCard, and Visa) that require consumers to insert the card into a reader rather than swiping it. This shift is intended to combat card fraud, which has more than doubled in the U.S. over the last 15 years while declining in regions that adopted chip technology earlier. The microchips function like mini-computers, providing a unique code for every transaction and making it difficult for criminals to create counterfeit cards. According to Visa's Stephanie Erickson, countries that transitioned to this technology within two years saw counterfeit fraud reduce by 60 to 70 percent. While the system offers increased security, it involves a trade-off in processing speed; "dipping" a chip card takes between five to ten seconds, compared to the three to five seconds required for a swipe. This increase in time may impact checkout lines during high-traffic periods. Liability for fraudulent transactions will shift based on specific deadlines. Starting October 1, merchants who have not upgraded their payment terminals to accommodate chips will be held responsible for fraud, while American Express will shift liability on October 16. However, federal law ensures that banks must still reimburse consumers for fraud. The rollout of chip-enabled cards is ongoing. While many customers at major banks like JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America are expected to receive new cards by the end of the year, some may not receive them until 2016. Existing cards without chips will remain functional until they expire or are replaced, but new terminals will prompt users to use the chip device instead of the swipe option.

Tami Erin, the actress who portrayed Pippi Longstocking in "The New Adventures of Pippi Longstocking," was arrested on a Saturday afternoon in Los Angeles for felony hit and run and driving under the influence (DUI). Law enforcement sources state that Erin allegedly hit three separate cars in a sequence of incidents, with at least one person requiring hospitalization. During the arrest, Erin was described as "totally out of control" and refused a blood alcohol test. Her bail was set at $100,000, and she has since been released from jail. Separately, Columbia Pictures, the studio behind the Pippi Longstocking film, initiated legal action against multiple websites using images from the 1988 movie to promote a sex tape featuring Erin. The studio's lawyers issued a warning to remove the images, leading the websites to remove DVD images and purchase links while retaining some Pippi references and a screenshot from the original movie. One website added a disclaimer stating the sex tape is not affiliated with the studio. The websites in question average over 200,000 views per day, with the majority of traffic originating from Sweden.

California Polytechnic State University (Cal Poly) recently lifted a ban on freshman fraternity recruitment, reversing a 2010 policy established after the death of freshman Carson Starkey. Starkey died in 2008 from alcohol poisoning during a Sigma Alpha Epsilon initiation ritual involving high-proof liquor. While some university leaders and parents argue that delaying recruitment protects vulnerable freshmen from binge drinking and hazing, the North-American Interfraternity Conference (NIC) and various student leaders campaigned to restore immediate recruitment, citing the importance of fraternity leadership, networking, and social support. Data indicates that freshmen account for 40 percent of the 60 fraternity-related deaths recorded nationwide since 2005. Research suggests that the first few months of college are a period of high vulnerability for binge drinking. Conversely, fraternity advocates argue that deferred recruitment is a "Band-Aid" on a broader campus problem and that the fraternity experience provides essential development for young men. The fraternity industry has developed sophisticated risk-management protocols, primarily through the Fraternal Information and Programming Group (FIPG). These protocols include "BYO" systems with strict guest lists and "sober monitors" to manage liability. However, critics and legal experts argue these policies are designed more to insulate national organizations from liability than to ensure student safety. In cases of serious injury or death, these protocols often involve crisis-management plans that prioritize the organization's legal standing, sometimes resulting in the exclusion of the individual student from insurance coverage and legal defense. Legal actions against fraternities have increased as incidents of alcohol poisoning, falls from heights, and hazing move into the public record. For example, multiple students at the University of Idaho and Washington State University suffered severe injuries or deaths from falls from fraternity house windows and roofs. While some argue that these are isolated accidents, others point to a recurring pattern of unsafe infrastructure and lack of adult supervision. The debate continues between those who view fraternities as a foundational leadership experience and those who view the industry as a high-risk environment where corporate risk management often takes precedence over student safety.

Hurricane Irene, a Category 3 storm with 115-mph winds, is moving north toward the U.S. East Coast after passing through the Bahamas. Forecasters predict the storm will make landfall along North Carolina's Outer Banks, specifically between Morehead City and Cape Hatteras, before moving into the Northeast. While some models suggest it could intensify into a Category 4 storm, others indicate it may weaken to a Category 2 as it reaches Virginia, Maryland, and Delaware. The storm is expected to produce 6 to 10 inches of rain in some areas, with up to 15 inches in others, and generate tidal surges of 5 to 10 feet. Multiple states, including North Carolina, Virginia, Maryland, New Jersey, New York, Delaware, and Connecticut, have declared states of emergency. In North Carolina, Governor Bev Perdue declared an emergency for all counties east of Interstate 95, and President Obama expedited federal assistance. Mandatory evacuations were issued for the Outer Banks and Ocean City, Maryland, leading to a significant exodus of tourists and residents. In New York City, Mayor Michael Bloomberg ordered the evacuation of low-lying hospitals and nursing homes and warned residents to stay out of parks and backyards due to the risk of falling trees. Transportation and infrastructure are being heavily impacted. Amtrak canceled all train service south of Washington D.C. for the weekend, and airlines reported expected flight delays and cancellations. In New York, officials prepared to shut down the city's mass transit system on Saturday. Utilities are preparing for widespread power outages, with repair crews from multiple states being mobilized. In the Washington region, Metro crews are deploying sandbags and chainsaws to maintain service. The storm has caused significant disruption to public events and local life. The dedication of the Martin Luther King Jr. National Memorial in Washington, D.C., was postponed indefinitely due to safety concerns. Colleges, including the University of Maryland and George Washington University, adjusted move-in schedules for the fall semester. In Florida, at least eight people were injured by a riptide caused by the storm's outer bands. Economic estimates suggest the hurricane could cause up to $13.9 billion in insured losses and $20 billion in overall economic damage.

Researchers have developed a method to produce an alcoholic beverage from used coffee grounds, resulting in a spirit with 40% ethanol content, which is comparable to hard liquors like vodka and tequila. The process involved collecting used grounds from a Portuguese coffee roasting company, drying them, and heating the powder in water at 163°C for 45 minutes. After separating the liquid and adding sugar, the team introduced yeast cells to ferment the mixture before concentrating the sample to increase the alcohol content. This method is similar to the processes used to create whiskey and rum from wheat and molasses. To evaluate the product, eight trained taste testers rated the beverage, describing it as having a coffee smell and a bitter, pungent taste. Researchers noted that the flavor could improve with age and concluded that the quality was sufficient for consumption. Additionally, the beverage is unlikely to contain significant caffeine because most of it is removed during the brewing process. The findings are scheduled to be reported in the September issue of LWT - Food Science and Technology.

Pope Francis expressed profound shock and sadness following a violent attack in Aden, Yemen, that killed 16 people, including four Catholic nuns. The victims were residents and staff at a retirement home for approximately 80 elderly people run by the Missionaries of Charity, a congregation founded by Mother Teresa. The four nuns were identified as being from Rwanda (two), India, and Kenya. On Friday, gunmen gained access to the facility by pretending to visit their mothers. They moved from room to room, handcuffing the victims before shooting them in the head. The attack occurred while the nuns were serving breakfast to the residents. The nun in charge managed to hide and escaped the violence unharmed. While no group has officially claimed responsibility for the massacre, Yemeni officials have attributed the attack to the Islamic State. The incident occurred amidst a brutal civil war between Iran-backed Houthi rebels in the north and the Saudi-Arabia-backed government in the south, a conflict that has resulted in more than 6,000 deaths and 2.4 million displaced people. In a message released by Vatican Secretary of State Cardinal Pietro Parolin, Pope Francis described the event as "senseless and diabolical violence" and a "pointless slaughter." The Pope offered prayers for the deceased and their families, calling on all parties in the Yemeni conflict to renounce violence, change their hearts, and pursue dialogue to serve the people of Yemen.

Delia Garcia-Bratcher, a 30-year-old woman from Santa Rosa, was arrested on Saturday for felony child abuse and inflicting injury on a child following an incident at Olivet Charter Elementary School. On Friday, Garcia-Bratcher entered the 320-student campus without checking in with the office to confront a 12-year-old boy she suspected of bullying her 10-year-old daughter. According to authorities, she approached the boy in front of other students, grabbed him by the throat, and shoved him. School staff took photographs of red marks and fingerprints on the boy's neck, which were still visible when deputies arrived. Garcia-Bratcher was released from jail on $30,000 bail and is scheduled to appear in court on Thursday. While she claimed she was on campus to enroll a third child and was seeking justice for her daughter, Sonoma County sheriff's officials stated that investigators have not yet substantiated the bullying allegations. Sheriff's Lt. Mark Essick noted that even if bullying were occurring, the woman's direct confrontation was "unreasonable" and "the wrong thing to do." The Piner-Olivet Union School District is investigating the bullying claims and drafting a letter to parents regarding the incident. Superintendent Jennie Snyder emphasized that the school has safety procedures in place, including requirements for visitors to sign in and wear badges, which Garcia-Bratcher did not follow. Garcia-Bratcher's telephone was disconnected, but she posted on a Facebook page expressing that she was hurt by the accusations and asserted that "the truth will be told."

Researchers at the University of Sussex and Colorado State University have discovered that African elephants (Loxodonta africana) can distinguish between different human languages and identify specific types of speakers. In a study conducted at Amboseli National Park in Kenya, researchers played recordings of the same phrase—"Look, look over there, a group of elephants is coming"—in both Maasai and Kamba languages. The elephants reacted defensively, gathering together and moving cautiously away, to the Maasai voices, which are associated with a group that occasionally kills elephants. They showed significantly less concern when hearing the voices of Kamba men, Maasai women, or young boys. The study, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, suggests that elephants possess sophisticated cognitive skills, including memory and the ability to decipher linguistic cues like inflections and vowels. The researchers noted that elephants were not fooled by altered voice pitches, indicating they recognize specific patterns rather than just tones. Furthermore, elephant groups with older matriarchs were more successful at assessing these threats, suggesting that this knowledge is learned and passed down. While the findings highlight the advanced intelligence and adaptability of elephants, experts noted that this ability reflects a troubling reality. Because elephants have developed specific alarm calls and can differentiate between human groups based on threat levels, it indicates that the conflict between humans and elephants is intensifying. This behavior suggests that elephants have categorized humans as a species to watch out for due to habitat loss and illegal hunting.

Actor Russell Crowe walked out of a BBC Radio 4 "Front Row" interview with Mark Lawson at the Dorchester Hotel in London on May 12, 2010. The confrontation occurred when Lawson suggested that Crowe's portrayal of Robin Hood in Ridley Scott's prequel film contained "hints" of an Irish accent. Crowe reacted defensively, telling the interviewer he had "dead ears" and calling the suggestion "ridiculous." During the exchange, Crowe used expletives and eventually refused to answer further questions regarding his role in the film or his performance in "Gladiator" before walking out while waving his cigarette. Crowe, a 46-year-old New Zealand-born actor, stated that he worked on his English accent by listening to tapes of Michael Parkinson to align with historical links to Yorkshire and Nottingham. While voice coaches aimed for an accent closer to the natives of Rutland, critics have offered mixed reviews, with some comparing his delivery to an Australian impression of Jim Bowen and others claiming he used 15 different accents. Crowe's history of temper includes a 2005 charge of second-degree assault in New York.

A Brooklyn grand jury has indicted 27-year-old probationary officer Peter Liang for the November 20, 2014, fatal shooting of 28-year-old Akai Gurley. The shooting occurred in a darkened stairwell of the Pink Houses in East New York while Gurley was visiting his girlfriend. Liang, who had been on the force for less than 18 months, was conducting a vertical patrol and allegedly discharged a 9-mm Glock while opening a door, striking Gurley in the chest. Liang faces charges of second-degree manslaughter, reckless endangerment, second-degree assault, and official misconduct. The indictment follows a brief grand jury session and occurs amid heightened national tension following previous grand jury decisions not to indict officers in the deaths of Michael Brown and Eric Garner. While Police Commissioner Bill Bratton characterized the shooting as an "unfortunate accident," critics and family members of the victim have described the incident as a reckless and wrongful shooting. Public response to the indictment has been mixed. Akai Gurley’s family and several political figures, including Representative Hakeem Jeffries, welcomed the move as a step toward justice. Conversely, police union leaders expressed concern for the officer's due process, citing the dangerous nature of the housing project where he was assigned. The case is notable for several distinctions from the Garner case: Liang did not testify before the grand jury, the grand jury heard evidence for only a few days, and the officer involved is Asian-American. Liang is expected to turn himself in for an arraignment in Brooklyn Supreme Court before Justice Danny Chun.

Ron and Mary Tarnowski, an elderly couple from Duluth, Minnesota, were found dead on August 5, 2024, following an eight-day search. The couple, aged 82 and 78 respectively, were last seen on July 29 at a store near Brookston, Minnesota. They had been missing for eight days before a U.S. Border Patrol helicopter located their Chevy Tahoe in a swampy area approximately one mile from their family’s hunting property. The couple's vehicle was found on a trail likely used only for snowmobiling. Mary Tarnowski was discovered inside the vehicle, while Ron Tarnowski was found approximately 50 feet from the car. Authorities suspect the vehicle became stuck in the mud, and Ron may have left the vehicle to seek help. While autopsy results are pending, family members and officials believe the couple died of exposure, specifically a combination of dehydration and heat during a day with temperatures in the upper 80s. No foul play is suspected. The Tarnowskis had been married for 59 years. Mary suffered from right-sided paralysis following a stroke decades ago, and Ron, an Air Force veteran and retired fire captain, had been her primary caretaker for over 35 years. In recent years, Ron had begun showing signs of early-stage dementia. The couple typically remained at home, but they had driven away on July 29 without notifying their children. They were not carrying cellphones or credit cards, and the vehicle's OnStar system was not activated, which hindered search efforts. The search involved a massive community effort, including hundreds of volunteers, local law enforcement, and retired firefighters. The community showed significant support through social media, news conferences, and the distribution of purple ribbons to raise awareness for Alzheimer's. The family emphasized the importance of using GPS tracking devices for vulnerable adults to prevent similar tragedies.

Pakistani television host Aamir Liaquat Hussain has sparked significant controversy by giving away abandoned babies to childless couples during live broadcasts of his Ramadan show, Aman Ramazan. During the holy month, Hussain presented two baby girls to unsuspecting families and planned to give away a baby boy in the following days. The infants were rescued by the Chhipa Welfare Association, an NGO that finds up to 15 abandoned babies a month in garbage bins and on the streets. While the Chhipa Welfare Association stated that the couples were vetted through several sessions, the families were not informed that they would receive a newborn until the live broadcast. The move has drawn mixed reactions. Some viewers and critics have condemned the act as a publicity stunt to win the television ratings battle, with some stating that babies are not trophies. Hussain defended the initiative, claiming it is a symbol of peace and love intended to provide homes for disenfranchised children who might otherwise become street kids or be involved in terrorism. He stated that his goal is to show an alternative to the violence and intolerance plaguing the nation. The controversy is set against a backdrop of intense competition for television ratings in Pakistan, where media outlets often use outrageous content to attract audiences. Hussain himself has a history of controversy, including accusations of using a fake university degree, resigning as a junior minister for religious affairs, and hosting scholars who called for the deaths of members of the persecuted Ahmadi sect. Because Pakistan does not have official adoption laws, the couples receiving the children must apply for guardianship through a family court.

U.S. Army Sgt. Adam Holcomb is the first of eight soldiers facing court-martial in connection with the suicide of 19-year-old Private Danny Chen at a remote combat outpost in Afghanistan on October 3. Prosecutors allege that Holcomb and other soldiers subjected the Chinese American private to merciless hazing, including racial slurs and physical abuse, leading to his death. Testimony from Pvt. Marcus Merritt revealed that Holcomb used racial slurs and threatened to kill him, contributing to Merritt’s own suicidal ideation. Other witnesses testified that Holcomb dragged Chen across rocks, causing back abrasions, after Chen failed to follow rules regarding a water heater. The defense argues that Chen committed suicide because his parents disowned him for joining the military and characterizes him as an incompetent soldier who required discipline for frequent violations. Defense lawyers also claimed that racially tinged nicknames were common "terms of endearment" within the tight-knit platoon. Conversely, some platoon members testified that while Chen’s failures justified "corrective training," the hazing was severe. The platoon medic reported that Chen denied having suicidal thoughts during a medical evaluation for his injuries. Holcomb faces charges of negligent homicide, assault, and reckless endangerment, with court-martials for the remaining seven soldiers scheduled for later this year.

Instagram co-founder Kevin Systrom announced that the company is reverting its recently updated privacy policy and terms of service to the original versions in effect since October 2010. The decision follows significant user backlash regarding proposed advertising changes and the potential use of user photos. Specifically, users expressed concern over a clause suggesting that Instagram could share personal data—such as favorite places and hobbies—with Facebook and advertisers, as well as a provision that would allow the company to use user photos in advertisements without compensation or knowledge. In his public statement, Systrom clarified that Instagram does not own user photos and has no intention of selling them. He acknowledged that the company failed to communicate its intentions clearly and stated that instead of seeking permission for future advertising products, the company will complete its plans first and then explain the business model to users. While the company is keeping the original advertising terms, it will still make small adjustments to the privacy policy to clarify how content is distributed. The controversy resulted in a notable migration of users to competing platforms. The app Pheed saw a surge in subscriptions and downloads, becoming the ninth most downloaded social-networking app on the Apple iTunes store. Flickr also saw a significant jump in its app rankings. High-profile entities, such as National Geographic, suspended their accounts in protest. While Instagram remains a dominant service with over 100 million members, the incident highlighted the volatility of the photo-sharing market and the pressure on Facebook to monetize the platform.

New Jersey lawmakers are considering a controversial bill aimed at reducing distracted driving by prohibiting any activity unrelated to the operation of a motor vehicle that interferes with safe driving. Sponsored by Democratic Assemblyman John Wisniewski and Nicholas Chiaravalloti, the legislation is modeled after a Maine law and seeks to move beyond current prohibitions on texting and hands-free devices to include activities such as eating, drinking, reading, and grooming. The proposed law carries significant penalties: first-offense violators could face fines between $200 and $400, second-offense violators between $400 and $600, and third-offense violators between $600 and $800, along with up to a 90-day license suspension and motor vehicle points. Wisniewski stated that the bill does not specifically mention coffee or food, but rather aims to discourage multitasking to prevent accidents. He noted that the legislation has faced significant public outcry, with critics often referring to it as the "ham sandwich bill" or "coffee bill." The legislation has faced criticism regarding its enforceability and necessity. Steve Carrellas of the National Motorists Association argued that the law is unnecessary because existing statutes already cover unsafe, careless, and reckless driving. He questioned how police would distinguish between illegal distractions and common actions like adjusting the radio or talking to passengers. Additionally, AAA officials expressed concern that the law may be difficult for police to enforce because an officer would need to observe the specific nature of the distraction. Supporters of the bill, including Arnold Anderson of the Essex County Community Traffic Safety Program, argue that the goal is to change the mindset of drivers who attempt to multitask. They suggest the law could function similarly to seat belt laws, where widespread messaging leads to ingrained behavioral changes even if enforcement is difficult. Federal data from 2014 cited by supporters highlights the severity of the issue, noting 400,000 accidents involving distracted drivers, 3,000 of which resulted in deaths.

Figure skaters Ryom Tae-Ok, 18, and Kim Ju-Sik, 25, became the first North Korean athletes to qualify for the 2018 Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang. The pair secured one of five available spots at the Nebelhorn Trophy in Oberstdorf, Germany, following a short program to music by The Beatles and a free skate performance to "Je suis qu'une chanson." The skaters trained in Montreal, Canada, under French coach Bruno Marcotte. While the International Skating Union confirmed their qualification, the final decision on their participation rests with the North Korean Olympic Committee. The skaters' coach, Kim Hyon-son, and Marcotte both noted that the decision is out of the athletes' hands. South Korean President Moon Jae-in stated that North Korean participation would provide an opportunity for inter-Korean peace and reconciliation, though he noted the North would be given until the last minute to decide. The qualification occurs amid heightened geopolitical tensions on the Korean peninsula. North Korean leader Kim Jong Un and U.S. President Donald Trump have exchanged war-like threats regarding North Korea's nuclear and missile programs. Historically, North Korea boycotted the 1988 Summer Olympics in Seoul. While the two nations have maintained convivial sporting relations this year through hockey and taekwondo events, the final decision on the skaters' Olympic presence remains uncertain.

Adrianne Haslet-Davis, a professional ballroom dancer who lost her left leg below the knee in the 2013 Boston Marathon bombing, is training to run the 120th Boston Marathon on April 18. She formed a team called "#AdrianneStrong" to raise funds for the Oklahoma City-based Limbs for Life Foundation, which provides limbs to those who cannot afford them. Haslet-Davis, who was with her husband when the second explosion occurred, previously vowed to return to dancing after the bombing. She fulfilled that goal in April 2015 by performing a fox-trot across the marathon finish line as part of an online campaign highlighting her recovery. While she was not a runner prior to the incident, she is currently training with trainers to become comfortable in her prosthetic blade, having reached 10 miles in her training. She dedicated her upcoming race to everyone who supported her and other survivors.

The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) enforces an "all or virtually all" standard for products marketed as Made in USA, requiring that all significant parts and processing be of U.S. origin. This standard applies to all products advertised or sold in the U.S. unless specifically governed by other laws. The FTC considers factors such as final assembly location, the percentage of total manufacturing costs attributable to U.S. parts, and how far removed foreign content is from the finished product. In contrast, "Assembled in USA" claims are appropriate for products where the principal assembly takes place in the U.S. and the last substantial transformation occurs domestically, even if some components are imported. The FTC does not pre-approve claims, but manufacturers must have a "reasonable basis" to substantiate any U.S. origin representation. While the U.S. Customs Service requires imported goods to be marked with a foreign country of origin based on the last "substantial transformation," the FTC maintains jurisdiction over deceptive marketing that goes beyond these requirements. Other federal statutes, such as the Textile Fiber Products Identification Act and the American Automobile Labeling Act, impose specific labeling requirements for textiles, wool, and automobiles. Recent reports by the watchdog group Truth in Advertising (TINA) identified over 100 products on Walmart’s website incorrectly labeled as "Made in the USA" despite being imported from countries like China and Germany. Walmart responded by stating that some manufacturing for store brands had recently moved to the U.S. and that website errors were due to outdated information or supplier-provided data. Walmart is currently conducting a quality assurance review to correct these coding errors. Additionally, the TINA report highlighted that Walmart’s website fails to clearly distinguish between "Made in the USA" and "Assembled in the USA" labels due to small font sizes and low contrast, which conflicts with the FTC's clear differentiation between the two standards.

Nancy Motes, the 37-year-old half-sister of actress Julia Roberts, died from an apparent drug overdose on Sunday in a Los Angeles home. Motes, a former production worker on the television show Glee, was found dead in a bathtub by her fiancé, John Dilbeck. While official toxicology results are pending and the coroner's office has deferred the cause of death pending further investigation, authorities recovered both prescription and non-prescription drugs at the scene. The death has sparked significant controversy and conflict between Motes' fiancé and the Roberts family. Conner Dilbeck, John’s brother, claimed in interviews that Motes took her life in a deliberate attempt to harm Julia Roberts' career and her chances of winning an Oscar for the film August: Osage County. He alleged that Motes left an eight-page suicide note containing "dark secrets" about Roberts and accused Roberts of inflicting "pure cruelty" on her sister. These claims are contested by sources who argue that timing a suicide to impact an Oscar nomination is illogical. Reports indicate a long-standing, strained relationship between the sisters. Roberts and their mother, Betty Lou Motes, reportedly attempted to get Motes into rehab for years, but she repeatedly refused. Motes had publicly criticized Roberts on social media and in tabloid interviews, accusing the actress of bullying her about her weight and being "cruel." Conversely, sources close to Roberts state that the actress was devastated by the loss and had tried in vain to save her sister from addiction. Legal and personal tensions persist regarding Motes' estate. John Dilbeck intends to be the sole beneficiary of her belongings, which includes personal items and photos given by Roberts. Some sources express concern that Dilbeck may use Motes' personal diaries or photos to publicize private family matters. Roberts, who canceled several professional commitments following the death, has remained largely silent publicly, while her representative released a statement confirming the family is "shocked and devastated."

Anthony Scaramucci, the newly appointed White House communications director, has publicly threatened to involve the FBI and the Justice Department following the leak of his financial disclosure information. The disclosure, which appeared on Politico's website, revealed that Scaramucci holds assets worth as much as $85 million, including a minority stake in the New York Mets and various real estate investments. The filing showed that he earned more than $5 million in salary and $4.9 million from his ownership stake in the investment firm SkyBridge Capital between January 1, 2016, and June 30, 2016. Scaramucci's appointment was delayed due to the complexities of the SkyBridge Capital sale to RON Transatlantic and the Chinese conglomerate HNA Group. While Scaramucci claimed his start date would be delayed until August to ensure his holdings were "cleansed," he has been on unpaid leave from the Export-Import Bank since June 19. The sale of SkyBridge is currently under review by the interagency Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States to ensure there are no risks to national security. In his social media posts, Scaramucci characterized the leak of his financial data as a felony and tagged White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus, who had previously opposed Scaramucci's appointment. Scaramucci remains an employee of SkyBridge Capital, though he stepped down from the executive post in January. He also reported earning $200,000 from a majority stake in Hastings Capital Group, which is also being sold as part of the SkyBridge deal, and $88,000 as a contributor to Fox News.

Hillary Clinton is not considering Bernie Sanders as a running mate for her presidential campaign, according to a Democrat familiar with the decision. Sanders, who has not officially conceded the Democratic primary to Clinton, did not expect to be considered for the vice presidency and does not view the decision as a slight. A person close to Sanders stated that he remains committed to changing the Democratic Party rather than becoming Clinton's partner, noting that joining her ticket would risk him being viewed as a sell-out by loyal supporters. Sanders did not raise the issue during a 90-minute private meeting with Clinton on Tuesday night. Separately, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996. This data is added to the Wayback Machine daily following an embargo period.

A study published in the journal Cancer reveals that one in seven colorectal cancer patients in the United States is diagnosed before the age of 50. Researchers from the University of Michigan analyzed data from the National Cancer Institute's SEER Program involving approximately 258,000 patients diagnosed between 1998 and 2011. The findings indicate a significant rise in cases among younger people, with projections suggesting that by 2030, nearly one in four rectal tumors and more than one in 10 colon cancers will be diagnosed in individuals under 50. Younger patients are more likely to be diagnosed with advanced malignancies, including regional and distant metastases. Specifically, those under 50 were 30 percent more likely to have cancer that had spread to lymph nodes and 50 percent more likely to have cancer that had reached other organs compared to older patients. Despite these advanced diagnoses, younger patients generally experienced slightly longer periods without cancer recurrence and showed higher five-year survival rates across all categories: 95.1 percent for localized disease, 76 percent for regional disease, and 21.3 percent for distant disease, compared to 91.9 percent, 70.3 percent, and 14.1 percent for older patients, respectively. Younger patients also underwent more aggressive treatments, with 72 percent receiving surgery and 53 percent receiving radiation, compared to 63 percent and 48 percent for older patients. The study highlights a potential gap in current screening guidelines, which recommend starting at age 50. Researchers suggest that nearly 15 percent of patients may be caught late due to these guidelines. However, experts remain divided on whether to lower the screening age. While some suggest using clinical judgment for young patients with symptoms like anemia, dark blood, or changes in bowel habits, others caution that early screening for low-risk younger populations could lead to unnecessary tests and high costs. Additionally, the study noted limitations such as a lack of family history data and potential selection bias. Experts also noted that while the increase in cases among younger adults is unexplained, environmental factors like obesity, smoking, and poor diet are known risk factors.

The Wisconsin Air National Guard's 115th Fighter Wing is conducting nighttime training flights from Monday through Thursday, with F-16 fighter jets expected to take off or land until approximately 10 p.m. These nighttime operations are part of readiness training for pilots and maintenance personnel, and flight paths are designed to minimize noise for residents. Separately, a "frostquake," or cryoseism, occurred in southern Wisconsin on Tuesday night due to freezing temperatures. The event, which was reported as a loud boom and brief rumbling felt in areas including Columbus, Fox Lake, Johnson Creek, Oconomowoc, and Beaver Dam, was caused by water draining into the ground, freezing, expanding, and fracturing the surrounding soil and rock. The National Weather Service confirmed the event, noting that while frostquakes are relatively rare, they are not unheard of in Wisconsin. No damage was reported.

Following a 9.0-magnitude earthquake and 10-meter tsunami on March 11, 2011, Japan is facing a severe nuclear crisis at the Fukushima Dai-ichi plant. Four of the six reactors have experienced fires, explosions, or partial meltdowns. Efforts to stabilize the site involve engineers attempting to restore power to cooling systems, while the military has used helicopters and high-pressure trucks to spray water on overheated fuel rods, particularly in Unit 3, which contains mixed oxide fuel (MOX) containing uranium and plutonium. The crisis has been elevated to level 5 on the International Nuclear Event Scale (INES), comparable to the 1979 Three Mile Island accident. While radiation levels in Tokyo have been deemed non-hazardous by health officials, the disaster has caused significant public anxiety, leading to an exodus of residents and tourists from the capital. The humanitarian toll is substantial, with over 6,400 confirmed dead and more than 10,000 missing. Approximately 452,000 people are currently in shelters, facing shortages of food, water, and medicine amid a cold snap. International responses have highlighted differing assessments between the U.S. and Japan. The U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission recommended a 50-mile evacuation zone, significantly larger than the 12-mile zone established by Japan. U.S. officials expressed concern over the status of spent fuel pools, suggesting some may be dry or nearly empty, potentially exposing fuel rods to air and causing them to overheat. To gather independent data, the U.S. deployed Global Hawk and U-2 aircraft to monitor the site. The Japanese government has faced criticism for its slow release of information. While the utility, Tokyo Electric Power Co. (TEPCO), reported progress in connecting external transmission lines to the plant, concerns remain regarding whether the battered cooling infrastructure is still functional. If cooling cannot be restored, authorities are considering a last resort of burying the plant in sand and concrete, a method previously used during the 1986 Chernobyl disaster. Global financial markets were also impacted, with the G7 nations intervening to stabilize the surging yen.

A federal judge in Camden, New Jersey, sentenced three men to prison for their roles in a scheme to cash money orders stolen from the U.S. Postal Service (USPS). Eugene Bowen, 36, of Knoxville, Tennessee, received 20 months in prison and three years of supervised release. Andre Sutton, 39, received nine months in prison, and Anthony Bell, 39, of Philadelphia, was sentenced to one year and a day in custody. The men were ordered to pay restitution totaling approximately $45,600, while the scheme's organizer, Marc Saunders, was sentenced to five years of probation, eight months of home confinement, and ordered to pay nearly $100,000 in restitution. The scheme involved stolen money orders produced using an imprinting machine stolen from the New Lisbon, New Jersey, post office. The theft was made possible by a postmaster's failure to return the machine or destroy 180 loose money orders during a technology transition in 2013. Saunders, a former employee at that branch, used the stolen machine to produce money orders with various ZIP codes to divert attention from the original location. Bowen admitted to recruiting individuals, including residents of homeless shelters in Knoxville, to cash the orders for a fee while keeping the remaining money for himself and Saunders. Investigators from the USPS Office of the Inspector General identified the participants by tracing serial numbers and linking social media and cellphone records. The investigation revealed that the accomplices shared personal and family ties; Bowen and Saunders are cousins, and Sutton grew up in the same neighborhood as Bell and attended high school with Saunders. The federal investigation began in late 2015 after stolen orders were cashed in the Philadelphia area, eventually leading to the discovery of the theft in Pemberton Township.

The concept of "sober January" is criticized by some as a practice rooted in guilt rather than science, potentially creating a dangerous illusion of sobriety for individuals with actual alcohol dependency problems. While the UK’s Alcohol Concern promotes the challenge, they include a disclaimer that it should not be attempted by those with alcohol dependency. Critics argue that the "all-or-nothing" nature of the challenge appeals to addicts and can be used as a tool for denial. Instead of a month-long abstinence, some suggest using Bill Wilson’s "alcoholism test," which involves attempting controlled drinking and stopping abruptly to determine if an individual has a dependency. The author, a former alcoholic who has been sober for several years, suggests that those worried about their drinking should focus on honest self-assessment rather than temporary abstinence.

A German Wehrmacht Enigma I cipher machine, manufactured in 1941, was sold at an auction at the Artmark auction house in Bucharest, Romania, for 45,000 euros ($51,500) to an unnamed online bidder. The machine was originally purchased by a cryptography professor for 100 euros ($114) at a local flea market. The machine was in almost perfect condition and was used by the Nazi German military during World War II to encrypt messages. The Enigma code was famously cracked by a team of cryptologists at Bletchley Park in southern England, led by mathematician Alan Turing. This breakthrough is widely credited with shortening the war by at least two years. While Romania was an ally of Nazi Germany until 1944, historians suggest the country may host other undiscovered cryptographic machines. In a separate sale last month, Christie's New York Books set a world auction record of $547,500 for a 1944 four-rotor Enigma cipher machine. Additionally, the University of Winnipeg Library, in collaboration with the University of Manitoba Libraries, the National Centre of Truth and Reconciliation, and the Library and Archives of Canada, is conducting an ongoing web-archiving project. Supported by Manitoba Tourism, Culture, Heritage, Sport and Consumer Protection, and the Government of Canada, the project captures webpages, blogs, news coverage, and PDF files regarding Manitoba's involvement with the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, including survivors’ stories, apologies, and cultural events.

Scientists at Oregon Health and Science University (OHSU) have successfully harvested stem cells from cloned human embryos, achieving a long-standing goal in regenerative medicine. Led by Shoukhrat Mitalipov, the team used somatic-cell nuclear transfer (SCNT)—a process similar to that used to clone Dolly the sheep—to create patient-specific embryonic stem cells (ESCs). The researchers achieved this by fusing a donor cell (such as skin cells) with an unfertilized egg, which then "reprograms" the DNA into an embryonic state. The success was attributed to refining technical steps, including the use of inactivated Sendai virus, electric jolts, and caffeine to protect eggs from premature activation. The study produced cell lines from fetal skin cells and skin cells from an 8-month-old patient with Leigh syndrome, proving that mature donor cells can be used to create stable cell lines. The process was efficient, requiring only 5 to 15 eggs per cell line. While the achievement is considered a landmark step toward creating DNA-matched tissues for treating diseases like Parkinson’s and diabetes, it faces significant hurdles. Because the process involves destroying human embryos, it is ethically controversial and ineligible for funding from the U.S. National Institutes of Health (NIH). The achievement also reignites the debate over the necessity of therapeutic cloning. Many researchers have shifted toward induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells, a technique that reprograms adult cells without embryos or eggs. While iPS cells are technically simpler, some experts note they may have molecular differences compared to SCNT-derived cells. Mitalipov argues that SCNT offers a specific advantage for treating certain rare mitochondrial diseases by providing new mitochondrial genes from the egg. To address concerns regarding "cloning hysteria," Mitalipov stated that his technique cannot produce cloned babies, a claim he intends to prove in upcoming publications.

A gang of thieves stole over £2 million worth of rare books from a warehouse in Feltham, Middlesex, on the night of January 29, 2017. The heist, which was captured on CCTV, involved thieves climbing onto the roof, cutting holes through reinforced skylights, and abseiling 40 feet into the building. The criminals targeted four specific containers, checking the contents against a list and ignoring other items in the warehouse. They then placed the books into holdalls, pulled them onto the roof using ropes, and drove away in a van. The stolen haul included more than 160 rare books, including a 1566 edition of Copernicus' "De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium" valued at approximately £215,000, as well as works by Galileo, Isaac Newton, Leonardo da Vinci, and a 1569 edition of Dante’s "Divine Comedy." The books were being stored temporarily in the warehouse before being flown to the United States for the 50th California International Antiquarian Book Fair. The Metropolitan Police are investigating the commercial burglary under crime reference number 0502127/17. The International League of Antiquarian Booksellers has published lists of the stolen items, identifying dealers from Italy and Germany as victims. One dealer from Padua, Italy, stated the robbery appeared to be a specialized "to order" job. However, art lawyer Chris Marinello refuted the "stolen to order" theory, suggesting the thieves may have used leaked inside information. Experts note that while publicity makes the books harder to sell through reputable dealers, they may be broken up into parts or sold at a fraction of their value on the underground market.

Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump accused Amazon founder Jeff Bezos of using his ownership of The Washington Post to influence corporate tax policy and protect Amazon from antitrust scrutiny. During an interview with Sean Hannity, Trump claimed that Bezos is using the newspaper as a "toy" for political power to prevent politicians from taxing Amazon. Trump further alleged that Bezos has assigned more than 20 reporters to investigate his life and predicted that any resulting book would be based on "false stuff." These comments followed a disclosure by Washington Post Associate Editor Bob Woodward, who stated that the newspaper has assigned 20 reporters to investigate Trump’s real estate deals. Woodward noted that Bezos had urged the Post to run extensive series on all presidential candidates to ensure voters are informed. While Bezos has donated to both Democratic and Republican officials in the past, including a $2.5 million donation for a 2012 gay marriage referendum and a recent $2,700 donation to a Republican representative, neither Bezos nor Amazon provided an immediate comment regarding Trump's specific accusations.

The leak of illegally obtained nude photos of dozens of celebrities, referred to as #CelebGate or "the Fappening," has resulted in the discovery of child pornography on the Reddit forum r/TheFappening. Specifically, photos of Olympic gymnast McKayla Maroney were confirmed by her lawyers to have been taken while she was under 18. Maroney's attorneys have issued demands to websites like Porn.com to remove the images, citing both the underage nature of the photos and her ownership of the copyright. Additionally, the forum has been used to share revenge-porn of actress Liz Lee, which was leaked by her boyfriend when she was 16. Reddit administrators have hidden the r/TheFappening subreddit from the site's main content list but have not yet banned the community, leaving moderators to manage the removal of illegal content. Within the subreddit, users have been observed providing instructions on how to use software to wipe hard drives containing the illegal images, while some users have expressed concern over the potential legal ramifications of possessing child pornography. In a contrasting development, members of the subreddit have raised $5,273 for the Prostate Cancer Foundation in honor of actress Jennifer Lawrence, making "Reddit The Fappening" the top donor for the foundation's $1 Million Matching Grant Challenge. While the source of the leaks remains unclear, Apple has denied reports that hackers accessed the photos through an iCloud vulnerability.

"Avengers: Infinity War" continues its box office dominance, earning $61.8 million in its third weekend at the domestic box office. This brings its total domestic earnings to $547.8 million and its global gross to over $1.6 billion, making it the fifth-highest-grossing film of all time. The film surpassed several major titles, including "The Avengers," "Furious 7," "Avengers: Age of Ultron," "Black Panther," and "Star Wars: The Last Jedi." On Saturday, it became the second-fastest film to reach the $500 million mark domestically. Two female-led releases benefited from a Mother's Day weekend boost. Melissa McCarthy’s comedy "Life of the Party" debuted in second place with $18.5 million from 3,656 locations, marking one of the lowest openings for a McCarthy-starrer. Gabrielle Union’s thriller "Breaking In," a low-budget production costing approximately $6 million, opened in third place with $16.5 million from 2,537 locations. Other notable performances included "Overboard," which earned $10 million in its second weekend (a 31% drop), and "A Quiet Place," which secured fifth place with $6.3 million. "A Quiet Place" showed strong longevity, declining only 18% in its sixth weekend to reach a domestic total of $169 million. Year-to-date box office figures are up 4.9% from 2017, with upcoming releases "Deadpool 2" (May 18) and "Solo: A Star Wars Story" (May 25) expected to provide further boosts.

France has seen a notable increase in cases of infanticide over the past three decades, leading experts to investigate the phenomenon of pregnancy denial. This psychiatric condition occurs when a woman either does not realize or cannot accept that she is pregnant, sometimes to the extent that physical manifestations like weight gain or breast enlargement are psychologically ignored. In these instances, the infant is viewed as an inconvenience to be discarded rather than an individual being. Several high-profile cases have been documented in France since 2003. In Villiers-au-Tertre, Dominique Cottrez was charged with murdering eight infants, some of which were found buried in gardens. Other notable cases include Véronique Courjault, who was jailed for murdering three babies and keeping their bodies in a freezer, and Céline Lesage, who was convicted of murdering seven babies after hiding her pregnancies from the fathers. Other historical cases include Jean-Pierre Leymarie and Rolande Leymarie, who were jailed for killing seven newborns in 1984, and Rosita Garnier, who was jailed for murdering three newborns. Experts offer varying perspectives on the causes of these crimes. Michel Delcroix describes pregnancy denial as a quasi-schizophrenic condition that may be triggered by past trauma, such as rape or beatings, or by shifting social factors that devalue parenting. He argues that because the condition acts as a psychotic state, these women should be treated as ill rather than simply punished. Psychiatrist Pierre Lamothe suggests that some women may want to feel feminine through pregnancy without the intention of procreation, leading them to fail to psychologically acknowledge the child's existence. While the condition is rising in frequency, experts note that while many women discover their pregnancies only at birth, only a fraction result in infanticide.

Public appearances by President Donald Trump and First Lady Melania Trump have drawn significant attention due to perceived tension and awkward interactions between the couple. During a trip to Israel in 2017, the couple was captured on camera at the Tel Aviv airport where the President reached for Melania’s hand, and she appeared to swat it away. This moment went viral, sparking online debate and concern. Similar incidents occurred during the 2017 inauguration, where Melania appeared to lean away during dances and avoid holding the President's hand. Observers and media outlets have noted Melania’s seemingly miserable demeanor, with some sources claiming she is unhappy in her role as First Lady. These perceptions were further fueled by a verified personal Twitter account of hers liking a tweet that joked about the emotional distance between the couple, an action the communications director later clarified was unauthorized. While some critics view her reactions as signs of personal distress or a "flinch" from the President's touch, others interpret her behavior as a public display of disdain or a reaction to the surrounding crowd. In addition to the marital dynamics, John Dean described the atmosphere inside the Trump White House as a "nightmare" for staff and assistants, highlighting the uncertainty and potential jeopardy faced by those working in the administration. Meanwhile, the Internet Archive is conducting efforts to archive web pages and fix broken links, a separate technical initiative unrelated to the political events.

The Australian government commissioned the research agency GfK Bluemoon to identify a color specifically designed to discourage smoking by making tobacco packaging as unappealing as possible. After three months of studies involving over 1,000 smokers, Pantone 448 C, also known as "opaque couché," was selected. Described as a drab, dark brown or "sludge-like" hue, respondents associated the color with terms such as "death," "dirty," and "tar." Australia adopted this color for all tobacco packaging alongside graphic health warnings in 2012. Following this initiative, the United Kingdom, Ireland, and France also implemented "plain packaging" laws using the same color. Research from the Cochrane Tobacco Addiction Group suggests that standardized packaging may reduce smoking prevalence and increase quit attempts. Data from the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) indicates a long-term decline in smoking rates in Australia. Between 2001 and 2013, the national daily smoking rate for adults aged 14 and older fell from 24.3% to 12.8%. Significant decreases were noted among youth, with the proportion of 12-to-17-year-olds who had never smoked reaching 95% in 2013. Smoking prevalence remains higher in remote areas and among those with lower socioeconomic status. The Australian government has set a goal to reduce the national adult daily smoking rate to 10% by 2018. The use of Pantone 448 C is part of a broader history of using color psychology to influence behavior. For example, in the 1960s, a similar color was used in restrooms to discourage employees from taking long breaks. While some experts describe the color as off-putting due to its association with waste, others view it as a neutral, earthy, or muted tone.

The Chicago Cubs are tied 1-1 with the Cleveland Indians in the World Series following a 5-1 victory in Game 2. The series has been defined by the unexpected return of 23-year-old third baseman Kyle Schwarber, who returned to the lineup just months after tearing two knee ligaments in April. Despite having no hits in four at-bats in April and only one hit in the Arizona Fall League, Schwarber has hit .429 in the World Series, recording a double and two RBI singles. Cubs management and teammates have expressed astonishment at Schwarber's performance, with catcher David Ross calling it the "Legend of Kyle Schwarber." While some critics questioned the risk of his return, Cubs president of operations Theo Epstein and general manager Jed Hoyer noted that Schwarber's recovery was ahead of schedule. The team is currently evaluating whether Schwarber can play the outfield in Game 3 at Wrigley Field, as the Cubs will return to National League rules without a designated hitter. While doctors initially cleared him only for hitting and running the bases, the team is consulting medical staff to see if he can play left field. Schwarber’s teammates, including Kris Bryant and Anthony Rizzo, highlighted his intense work ethic during his rehabilitation, noting he remained mentally engaged with the team by watching film and studying scouting reports. He also wears a green wristband in honor of his 10-year-old friend, Campbell Faulkner, who has a rare mitochondrial disease. The Cubs are preparing for Game 3 on Friday, which will be the first World Series night game in the history of Wrigley Field.

Cassie Young, a 31-year-old digital director for "The Bert Show," recently shared a social media exchange that highlighted issues of body shaming and self-worth. After getting engaged to her boyfriend of nine years, Young was contacted by an unnamed personal trainer who offered to help her "shape up" for her wedding day. When Young declined the offer, stating she was happy with her appearance, the trainer persisted by claiming she could not be happy with her looks and suggesting she should hire someone to change her body for her wedding photos. Young responded by criticizing the trainer's focus on superficiality, stating that her self-worth is not defined by her appearance. She shared the screenshots on Facebook, where she received widespread support from users who condemned the trainer's tactics. Young, who has publicly shared her personal struggles with body acceptance and past experiences with calorie counting and yo-yo dieting, noted that the trainer’s comments were an attempt to plant seeds of doubt. Despite the interaction, Young chose not to publicly name the trainer, expressing a desire for the situation to serve as a learning opportunity rather than a means to vilify him for his ignorance.

The Internet Archive initiated a "Survey" crawl on February 24, 2018, using a seed list of 339,249,218 URLs that received a 200 response code in 2017. The crawl utilized a Heritrix setting of "maxHops=0" to include embedded URLs. These crawls are part of a broader effort to archive pages as they are created along with their referenced pages to preserve versions of content that may be changed or removed. The goal is to fix broken links on the web by providing archived versions of original content. The associated WARC files are not currently available to the general public. Separately, Dave Tavres, a software geek from Culver City and former Disneyland Railroad engineer, launched Mousemingle.com, the world's first all-Disney dating website. The site was conceived following a 2011 trip to Disneyland because Tavres found it difficult to find dates who shared his specific interest in Disney. Mousemingle.com features traditional dating site elements like customizable profiles and photos, but distinguishes itself by asking users about Disney songs, shopping habits, and "Disney nerd levels." The platform requires a $12.55 monthly membership fee for users to contact others via private messages or chats. Tavres aims to connect Disney fans globally, particularly those living in areas far from major theme parks.

Moses Malone, a three-time NBA MVP and iconic center for the Philadelphia 76ers, died in his sleep in Norfolk, Virginia, at the age of 60. Malone was a cornerstone of the 76ers' 1983 championship team, a title that marked the organization's first championship since 1967. He was known for his ferocious rebounding, leading the league in rebounds per game for five consecutive seasons from 1980 to 1985 and becoming the NBA's career leader in offensive rebounds. Acquired from the Houston Rockets in the summer of 1982, Malone’s arrival addressed a perceived lack of physical presence on a team that had been eliminated in the Finals three times and the Eastern Conference Finals twice in the preceding six years. In his first season with the Sixers, Malone won MVP honors, averaging 24.5 points and 15.3 rebounds during a 65-17 regular season. He led the team to a championship by sweeping the Los Angeles Lakers in the finals, where he averaged 26 points and 15.8 rebounds to earn Finals MVP. Malone's tenure with the Sixers included a notable contract—the largest for a player in team sports at the time—valued at $2.2 million per season over six years. He was later traded to the Washington Bullets in a controversial deal, only to return to the Sixers in 1993-94. Throughout his 19-season NBA career, he was a 12-time All-Star, won three MVPs, and was named to the league's 50th anniversary all-time team in 1995. He was inducted into the Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame in 2001. The 76ers organization and former teammates described Malone as a "genuine icon" and a "basketball junkie" who was highly respected for his intelligence and personality. He was also noted for his connection with fans, characterized by a "jovial smile and large laugh" off the court. Malone's passing follows the deaths of two other former Sixers centers, Darryl Dawkins and Caldwell Jones, who both died of heart attacks within the past year.
